note: Practice questions authored by Fly GACA from the GACAR library for study. Not actual GACA exam questions. Every answer cites its source — verify against the official GACAR.

banks.title: Commercial Pilot Licensing
banks.desc: CPL eligibility, aeronautical experience, privileges and limitations under GACAR Part 61.
banks.source: GACAR Part 61, Subpart F · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 61, the minimum age to be eligible for a commercial pilot certificate is —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.153(a) requires a commercial pilot certificate applicant to be at least 18 years of age.

questions.q: Before being issued a commercial pilot certificate, an applicant must already hold at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.153(e) requires the applicant to hold at least a private pilot certificate issued under Part 61 (or meet the requirements of §61.93).

questions.q: Which class of medical certificate must a commercial pilot certificate applicant hold (for airplane operations)?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.153(g) requires a Class 1 medical certificate for a commercial pilot certificate; only glider or balloon applicants may hold a Class 2.

questions.q: A commercial pilot certificate applicant needs only a Class 2 medical certificate when testing for or exercising the privileges in a —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.153(g) makes an exception to the Class 1 requirement: persons undergoing the practical test for or exercising commercial privileges in a glider or balloon need only a Class 2 medical certificate.

questions.q: Which pilot certificate is the minimum required to act as pilot in command of an aircraft carrying persons or property for compensation or hire?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.155(a)(2) grants the commercial pilot certificate holder the privilege to act as PIC carrying persons or property for compensation or hire, making it the minimum certificate for paid operations.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.155, a commercial pilot certificate holder may exercise the same privileges as a person holding a —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.155(a)(1) provides that a commercial pilot may exercise the same privileges as a private pilot, in addition to the compensation-or-hire privileges.

questions.q: A commercial pilot who applies with an airplane category rating and does not hold an instrument rating in the same category and class is limited from carrying passengers for hire on cross-country flights in excess of —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.155(b)(1) imposes the limitation that carriage of passengers for hire on cross-country flights in excess of 50 NM (or at night) is prohibited without the corresponding instrument rating.

questions.q: Besides cross-country flights over 50 NM, the no-instrument-rating commercial limitation also prohibits carrying passengers for hire —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.155(b)(1) prohibits carriage of passengers for hire on cross-country flights over 50 NM or at night until an instrument rating is obtained.

questions.q: How is the commercial pilot's 50 NM / night carriage-for-hire limitation removed?
options: By obtaining an instrument rating in the same category and class
By passing a separate night checkride
questions.explain: GACAR §61.155(b)(1) states the limitation may be removed once the person satisfactorily accomplishes the §61.89 requirements for an instrument rating in the same category and class listed on the certificate.

questions.q: GACAR §61.151 states that this subpart prescribes the requirements for the issuance of which certificates?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.151 (Applicability) provides that the subpart prescribes the requirements, conditions, privileges, and limitations for commercial pilot certificates and ratings.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.3, a person may not exercise the privileges of a certificate issued under Part 61 if that certificate is —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.3(a) prohibits exercising the privileges of a certificate, rating, endorsement, or authorization that has been surrendered, suspended, revoked, or expired.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.3, a commercial or airline transport pilot may not serve as a pilot in international operations under Part 121 or 135 after attaining their 60th birthday, or, in operations with more than one pilot, their —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.3(c) bars a CPL or ATP holder from serving as a pilot in such international operations after the 60th birthday, extended to the 65th birthday in multi-pilot operations.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.5, to exercise the privileges of a certificate a person must have in their physical possession or readily accessible in the aircraft —
options: A current and valid airman certificate
questions.explain: GACAR §61.5(a) requires a current and valid airman certificate (or §61.37(c) document / Temporary Flight Authorization) endorsed with the necessary ratings, in possession or readily accessible in the aircraft.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.9, the medical certificate that a required flight crew member must hold is issued under GACAR Part —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.9(a) requires a required flight crew member to hold the appropriate medical certificate issued under GACAR Part 67.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a pilot certificate issued under GACAR §61.7?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.7(a) lists the pilot certificates as student, sport, private, commercial, and ATP; aircraft dispatcher is not a pilot certificate under this part.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.7, instrument ratings are placed on which pilot certificates?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.7(b)(8) specifies that instrument ratings are placed on private and commercial pilot certificates only.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.153, the commercial pilot knowledge test is based on the aeronautical knowledge areas listed in an appendix to GACAR Part —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.153(b) requires passing the knowledge test on the aeronautical knowledge areas listed in Appendix C to GACAR Part 141.

questions.q: For an applicant taking the logged ground-and-flight-training route to a commercial pilot certificate, the required aeronautical experience is prescribed in which appendix to GACAR Part 61?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.153(c)(4) requires that such applicants meet the aeronautical experience requirements of Appendix D to Part 61.

banks.title: Commercial Air Operations
banks.desc: Air operator certification and the operating rules for commercial flights (GACAR Parts 119, 135, 91).

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 119, a person authorized to conduct operations as a commercial operator is issued which certificate?
questions.explain: GACAR §119.1 states that a person authorized to conduct operations as a commercial operator under Part 121 or 135 is issued an Air Operator Certificate (AOC); noncommercial operators under Part 125 receive an Operator Certificate (OC).

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 119, a person authorized to conduct noncommercial operations under GACAR Part 125 is issued —
questions.explain: GACAR §119.1 provides that a person authorized to conduct noncommercial operations under Part 125 will be issued an Operator Certificate (OC), as distinct from the AOC issued to commercial operators.

questions.q: GACAR Part 119 prescribes the certification requirements for operations conducted under which Parts?
questions.explain: GACAR §119.1 states that Part 119 prescribes the certification requirements an operator must meet to obtain and hold a certificate authorizing operations under Part 121, 125, or 135.

questions.q: According to GACAR §119.1, Part 119 does NOT apply to which of the following?
options: Foreign air carriers operating under GACAR Part 129
questions.explain: GACAR §119.1 expressly states that Part 119 does not apply to activities conducted by foreign air carriers under Part 129, nor to aerial work operations under Part 133.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.1, no person may operate as a commercial operator under Part 119 without also holding —
options: Appropriate economic authority from the GACA
questions.explain: GACAR §119.1 provides that no person may operate as a commercial operator under this part without holding appropriate economic authority from the GACA, in addition to the AOC and operations specifications.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.5, a certificate holder's operations specifications must contain —
options: The authorizations, limitations, and certain procedures under which each kind of operation is conducted
A list of passenger fares by route
The maintenance records of every engine
questions.explain: GACAR §119.5 requires that operations specifications contain the authorizations, limitations, and certain procedures under which each kind of operation is to be conducted, plus procedures for how each class of aircraft is to be operated.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.9, a certificate holder must not operate an aircraft under Part 121 or 135 using a business name other than —
options: The name of its principal pilot
A business name appearing in its operations specifications
Any name approved by the aircraft lessor
questions.explain: GACAR §119.9 prohibits a certificate holder from operating under a business name other than one appearing in its operations specifications.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.9, how must the certificate holder's business name relate to the aircraft it operates under Part 121 or 135?
options: It need not appear anywhere on the aircraft
It must be legibly displayed and clearly visible from outside the aircraft to a person on the ground
It must be shown only on the flight plan
It must be painted on the interior cabin only
questions.explain: GACAR §119.9 requires the operating certificate holder's business name to be legibly displayed on the aircraft and clearly visible and readable from outside to a person standing on the ground (except during flight time).

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.9, when a certificate holder operates a wet-leased aircraft under Part 121 or 135, whose business name must be legibly displayed on the aircraft?
options: No name is required for wet leases
questions.explain: GACAR §119.9 requires that for a wet-leased aircraft, the business name of the lessor be legibly displayed and clearly visible from outside the aircraft to a person standing on the ground.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.43, an application for an AOC may be denied if the President finds that —
options: The applicant has fewer than five aircraft
The applicant previously held an AOC or OC that was revoked
The applicant operates only within the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
The applicant intends to conduct only cargo operations
questions.explain: GACAR §119.43 lists grounds for denying a certificate, including that the applicant previously held an AOC or OC that was revoked, or is not properly and adequately equipped to conduct safe operations.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.43, before issuing an AOC the President must find that the applicant is properly and adequately equipped in accordance with the requirements of —
questions.explain: GACAR §119.43 requires the President to find that the applicant is properly and adequately equipped in accordance with GACAR Part 121 or 135, as applicable, and able to conduct a safe operation, before issuing an AOC.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.49, the operations specifications of a certificate holder conducting commercial operations must include —
options: The specific location of the certificate holder's principal base of operations
A copy of every passenger's ticket
The daily fuel prices at each aerodrome
questions.explain: GACAR §119.49 requires operations specifications to contain, among other items, the specific location of the certificate holder's principal base of operations, other business names, reference to the economic authority, and the aircraft authorized for use.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.37, to operate as a commercial operator a person must, among other things —
options: Be a citizen of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia and maintain a principal base of operations in the Kingdom
Own at least one transport-category aircraft
Be based outside the Kingdom for tax purposes
questions.explain: GACAR §119.37 provides that a person may not operate as a commercial operator unless they are a citizen of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, maintain a principal base of operations in the Kingdom, and obtain an AOC.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.39, an applicant for an AOC must submit the application to the President at least —
options: 30 working days before the date of intended operation
60 working days before the date of intended operation
90 working days before the date of intended operation
15 working days before the date of intended operation
questions.explain: GACAR §119.39 requires each applicant to submit the AOC application at least 90 working days before the date of intended operation.

questions.q: Under GACAR §119.21, a person conducting commercial operations using aircraft other than transport-category or commuter-category airplanes must comply with the certification requirements of Part 119 and —
questions.explain: GACAR §119.21 provides that commercial operators using transport-category aircraft or commuter-category airplanes follow Part 121, while those using aircraft not so addressed must comply with Part 119 and Part 135.

questions.q: GACAR §135.1 prescribes rules governing the commercial operations of —
options: Aircraft other than transport-category aircraft and commuter-category airplanes, by holders of an AOC under Part 119
questions.explain: GACAR §135.1 states that Part 135 governs the scheduled and unscheduled commercial operations of aircraft OTHER than transport-category aircraft and commuter-category airplanes, conducted by persons holding or required to hold an AOC under Part 119.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.7, in addition to the documents required by Part 91, the pilot in command must ensure which document is carried on board?
options: A certified true copy of the AOC and a copy of the operations specifications summary
The original design drawings of the aircraft
A copy of every crew member's pilot logbook
questions.explain: GACAR §135.7 requires the PIC to ensure that a certified true copy of the AOC and a copy of the operations specifications summary (and, when applicable, a copy of the Article 83 bis agreement) are carried on board.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.9, for how long must the flight logbook records be maintained?
questions.explain: GACAR §135.9 requires that flight logbook records be maintained for 30 days, and makes the PIC responsible for ensuring the entries are completed per the certificate holder's procedures.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.15, a single-engine airplane may be operated in instrument meteorological conditions (IMC) or at night only if, among other conditions —
options: The flight remains within 25 NM of the departure aerodrome
The certificate holder is authorized by the President to conduct such operations
At least two pilots are on board regardless of type
The airplane carries a parachute for each occupant
questions.explain: GACAR §135.15 permits single-engine airplane operations in IMC or at night only when several conditions are met, including that the certificate holder is specifically authorized by the President to conduct such operations.

questions.q: GACAR §91.1 establishes that Part 91 prescribes rules governing the operation of aircraft —
options: Only outside the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
Within the sovereign area and territorial waters of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
questions.explain: GACAR §91.1 states that Part 91 prescribes the general operating and flight rules for aircraft operated within the sovereign area and territorial waters of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, with certain exceptions.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.3, the pilot in command of an aircraft is —
options: Subordinate to the flight dispatcher in all decisions
Directly responsible for, and the final authority as to, the operation, safety, and security of that aircraft
Responsible only for navigation, not safety
questions.explain: GACAR §91.3 makes the PIC directly responsible for, and the final authority as to, the operation, safety, and security of the aircraft and the safety of all crew members, passengers, and cargo on board, and permits deviation from the rules to the extent required in an in-flight emergency.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.9, which of the following must the PIC ensure is carried on board a Saudi Arabian registered aircraft?
options: An original or true copy of the aircraft certificate of registration and the airworthiness certificate
A printed weather forecast for the next seven days
A signed copy of the certificate holder's business plan
questions.explain: GACAR §91.9 requires the PIC to ensure documents such as the aircraft certificate of registration, the airworthiness certificate, the radio station license, a passenger list, a cargo manifest, and the flight logbook are carried on board at all times.

banks.title: Commercial Performance & Loading
banks.desc: Weight, balance, performance and fuel requirements for commercial operations (GACAR Part 91 · 135).
banks.source: GACAR Part 91 · Part 135 (Subpart F) · DRAFT — pending review

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 91, an airplane may not begin a flight under VFR during the day unless it has enough fuel to reach the first point of intended landing and then fly at normal cruising speed for at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.161(a) requires, for a daytime VFR airplane flight, fuel to reach the first point of intended landing plus at least 30 minutes at normal cruising speed.

questions.q: For a night VFR flight in an airplane, GACAR §91.161 requires fuel to reach the first point of intended landing and then fly at normal cruising speed for at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.161(a) sets the night VFR airplane reserve at 45 minutes at normal cruising speed beyond the first point of intended landing (versus 30 minutes by day).

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.161, a rotorcraft may not begin a VFR flight unless it can reach the first point of intended landing and then fly at normal cruising speed for at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.161(b) sets a single 20-minute reserve for rotorcraft under VFR, regardless of day or night.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 91, an airplane operating under IFR must carry enough fuel to fly to and make an approach at the destination, fly to the alternate, and then fly at normal cruising speed for at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.181(a) requires an IFR airplane to carry fuel for the destination approach/missed approach, the flight to the alternate, and then 45 minutes at normal cruising speed.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.181, after reaching the alternate aerodrome an IFR rotorcraft must carry fuel to fly at normal cruising speed for a further —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.181(a)(3) gives rotorcraft a 30-minute final reserve at normal cruising speed under IFR, compared with 45 minutes for other aircraft.

questions.q: GACAR §91.43 (Preflight Action) requires the PIC, before a flight not in the vicinity of an aerodrome or under IFR, to become familiar with — among other things —
options: only the weather at the departure aerodrome
runway lengths at aerodromes of intended use and the applicable takeoff and landing distance data
nothing additional if the flight is conducted under VFR
questions.explain: GACAR §91.43(a) lists required preflight information including weather, fuel, alternatives, and runway lengths plus takeoff/landing distance data at aerodromes of intended use.

questions.q: Before a flight may commence under GACAR §91.43, the PIC must be satisfied that —
options: the aircraft mass and centre of gravity allow the flight to be conducted safely and any load is properly distributed and secured
the aircraft has flown within the preceding 30 days
the aircraft has been repainted within 12 months
questions.explain: GACAR §91.43(b) requires the PIC to confirm, before flight, that the aircraft's mass and CG permit safe flight, that any load is properly distributed and secured, and that operating limitations will not be exceeded.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.11, a Saudi-registered aircraft's current empty mass and centre of gravity must be derived from an actual weighing performed within the preceding —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.11(a) requires empty mass and CG to be calculated from an actual weighing within the preceding 36 months (unless an approved mass-and-balance control system or another exception applies).

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.267 (In-Flight Fuel Management), the PIC must declare a fuel emergency by broadcasting "MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY FUEL" when —
options: the aircraft has burned half of its planned trip fuel
the calculated usable fuel on landing at the nearest suitable aerodrome would be less than the planned final reserve fuel
the destination alternate is more than one hour away
questions.explain: GACAR §91.267 requires a MAYDAY FUEL emergency declaration when the calculated usable fuel predicted on landing at the nearest safe aerodrome is less than the planned final reserve fuel; a MINIMUM FUEL advisory precedes that state.

questions.q: Under GACAR §91.263, when an aircraft operating within controlled airspace needs to dump fuel, the PIC must —
options: advise ATC before commencing the fuel-dump operation
obtain written approval from the operator
questions.explain: GACAR §91.263 requires the PIC to advise ATC prior to commencing a fuel-dump operation when operating within controlled airspace.

questions.q: In GACAR Part 135, airplane performance operating limitations (takeoff, en-route, and landing limits) for commercial operators are prescribed in —
questions.explain: GACAR Part 135 Subpart F is titled "Airplane Performance Operating Limitations"; Subpart G covers the equivalent rotorcraft limitations.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.135, when ensuring an airplane's takeoff mass does not exceed the mass allowed for the runway length, the operator must consider at least aerodrome pressure altitude, wind, ambient temperature, runway slope, and —
options: runway contaminants such as standing water, snow, or ice
the time since the last engine overhaul
questions.explain: GACAR §135.135(f) lists the required factors — pressure altitude, wind, ambient temperature, runway slope, and runway contaminants (standing water, snow, or ice).

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.137 (Airplane: Takeoff Limitations), the accelerate-stop distance must not exceed —
options: the length of the runway plus any stopway
the runway length plus any clearway
questions.explain: GACAR §135.137(b)(1) requires the accelerate-stop distance to be no greater than the length of the runway plus the length of any stopway.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.137, the takeoff distance must not exceed the runway length plus any clearway, but the clearway credited must not be greater than —
questions.explain: GACAR §135.137(b)(2) allows the takeoff distance to include clearway, but the clearway credited may not exceed one-half of the runway length.

questions.q: For the takeoff limitations of GACAR §135.137, it is assumed the airplane is not banked before reaching a height of 50 ft (15 m), and thereafter the maximum bank does not exceed —
questions.explain: GACAR §135.137(d) assumes no bank before 50 ft (15 m) and a maximum bank of 15 degrees thereafter along the takeoff/net takeoff flight path.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.139 (Landing Limitations: Destination Aerodromes), takeoff mass must be limited so that, allowing for fuel and oil burn, the airplane can make a full-stop landing —
options: within the landing distance available of the most suitable runway at the destination
within 70 percent of any runway at the destination
using no more than half the runway in still air
regardless of wind direction on the longest runway only
questions.explain: GACAR §135.139(a) limits mass so the airplane can make a full-stop landing within the landing distance available of the most suitable runway at the destination aerodrome.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.139, if forecasts indicate the destination runways may be wet or slippery and no wet-runway AFM data is used, a turbojet-powered airplane requires an effective runway length of at least —
options: 100 percent of the dry-runway required length
115 percent of the dry-runway required length
70 percent of the dry-runway required length
150 percent of the dry-runway required length
questions.explain: GACAR §135.139(c) requires, for a turbojet landing on possibly wet/slippery runways without applicable AFM wet data, an effective runway length of at least 115 percent of that required by paragraph (a).

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.141, an aerodrome may not be listed as an alternate unless the airplane can land and stop within — of the effective runway length from a point 50 ft above the obstruction-clearance-plane/runway intersection.
questions.explain: GACAR §135.141 requires an alternate to permit a full-stop landing within 70 percent of the effective runway length, using the §135.139 assumptions.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.143, a multi-engine land airplane carrying passengers over water (beyond gliding distance of land) must be operated at a mass allowing it to climb, with the critical engine inoperative, at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §135.143(c) requires a multi-engine land airplane over water to be able to climb at least 50 ft/min (0.25 m/s) with the critical engine inoperative at 1,000 ft (300 m) AGL.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.145, a multi-engine airplane carrying passengers over the top or in IMC must be able to climb, with the critical engine inoperative, at least 50 ft/min when operating at —
options: the route MEA or 5,000 ft MSL, whichever is higher
the route MEA or 1,500 ft MSL, whichever is lower
questions.explain: GACAR §135.145(a) requires a 50 ft/min single-engine-inoperative climb capability at the minimum en-route IFR altitude (MEA) or 5,000 ft MSL, whichever is higher.

questions.q: Under GACAR §135.658 (Fuel and Oil Supply), the contingency fuel carried to compensate for unforeseen factors must be no less than —
options: 5 percent of the planned trip fuel (subject to a minimum holding allowance)
10 percent of the planned trip fuel
15 percent of the final reserve fuel
questions.explain: GACAR §135.658 sets contingency fuel at no less than 5 percent of the planned trip fuel (or from the point of in-flight re-planning), never below the fuel to fly 5 minutes at holding speed 1,500 ft above the destination.

banks.title: General Air Law
banks.desc: The structure of the GACARs, the regulator, and how Saudi aviation law fits together.

questions.q: The civil aviation regulator of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia is —
options: The General Authority of Civil Aviation (GACA)
questions.explain: The General Authority of Civil Aviation (GACA) is the State's civil aviation regulator and publishes the GACARs.

questions.q: Which GACAR Part contains the general operating and flight rules?
questions.explain: GACAR Part 91 — General Operating and Flight Rules — governs the operation of aircraft within KSA.

questions.q: Pilot certification and licensing requirements are set out in which GACAR Part?
questions.explain: GACAR Part 61 covers the certification of airmen — pilot licences, ratings and the associated requirements.

questions.q: The GACARs are written to comply, as far as practicable, with —
options: The Standards and Recommended Practices of ICAO
questions.explain: The stated goal of the GACAR is to comply, to the maximum extent practicable, with ICAO Standards and Recommended Practices; differences are published in the AIP.

questions.q: The GACAR Parts are numbered in the range —
questions.explain: The GACARs run from Part 1 through Part 199, grouped by subject area.

questions.q: A pilot holding a foreign (e.g. FAA or EASA) licence who wishes to fly on the Saudi register must —
options: Use the foreign licence directly with no further action
Convert it to, or obtain, a GACA licence
Only fly with a Saudi co-pilot
questions.explain: A foreign licence must be converted to — or a GACA licence otherwise obtained — to exercise privileges on the Saudi register.

questions.q: May a pilot in command deviate from a rule of GACAR Part 91 in an in-flight emergency?
options: Yes — to the extent required to meet that emergency
questions.explain: GACAR §91.3 allows the PIC, in an in-flight emergency requiring immediate action, to deviate from the rules to the extent required to meet that emergency.

questions.q: The authoritative, current source for any GACAR requirement is —
questions.explain: Only GACA's official publication is authoritative. Study tools and summaries must always be verified against the current official GACAR.

questions.q: Where ICAO Annex provisions are adopted into Saudi law, they take effect —
options: When adopted by reference within the GACARs
Only after a NOTAM is issued
questions.explain: ICAO Annex provisions apply in KSA where the GACARs adopt them — often by reference within a relevant Part.

questions.q: The topical handbook that explains the legal basis, ICAO compliance and scope of the GACARs is the —
questions.explain: The Foreword sets out the legal basis, historical background, ICAO compliance and applicability of the GACARs.

questions.q: Amendments to the GACARs and their effective dates are tracked in the —
questions.explain: The Change History document records GACAR amendments and the version in which each change took effect.

questions.q: GACAR Part 91 applies to aircraft operations —
options: Within the sovereign area and territorial waters of KSA
questions.explain: GACAR §91.1 — Part 91 prescribes rules governing the operation of aircraft within the sovereign area and territorial waters of the Kingdom.

questions.q: Which GACAR Part prescribes the rules governing the issuance, amendment and repeal of the GACARs?
questions.explain: GACAR §11.1 states that Part 11 prescribes the rules governing the issuance, amendment and repeal of the GACARs and the requirements for petitions for exemption.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 11, who may petition the President for an exemption from all or part of a regulatory requirement?
options: Only the GACA Board of Directors
questions.explain: GACAR §11.9 provides that any person may petition the President for an exemption from all or part of a regulatory requirement issued by the GACA.

questions.q: If a petition for exemption is denied, the petitioner may next file —
options: A petition for reconsideration with the President
A new application after five years
questions.explain: GACAR §11.13 allows a petitioner whose exemption was denied to file a petition for reconsideration with the President, based on an erroneous material fact or new relevant facts.

questions.q: Under GACAR §13.23, a person who knows of a violation of the Civil Aviation Law or a rule issued under it must —
questions.explain: GACAR §13.23 requires any person who knows of a violation of the Civil Aviation Law or any rule, regulation or order under it to report it to the President.

questions.q: Which remedial administrative action may the President issue to bring to a person's attention that an incident appears contrary to the regulations but does not warrant further enforcement?
questions.explain: GACAR §13.63 allows the President to issue a warning notice when the facts appear contrary to the regulations but do not warrant further compliance enforcement action.

questions.q: As a result of an investigation, the President may issue an order amending, suspending or revoking an airman certificate when —
options: The public interest and aviation safety require it
The certificate is more than five years old
questions.explain: GACAR §13.103 allows the President to issue an order amending, suspending or revoking a certificate when the public interest and aviation safety require it.

questions.q: "GACA" stands for —
questions.explain: GACA is the General Authority of Civil Aviation, the civil aviation regulator of Saudi Arabia.

questions.q: A GACAR "Part" is generally organized internally into —
options: Subparts, further divided into numbered sections
questions.explain: Each GACAR Part is divided into Subparts, which are further divided into numbered sections (e.g. §91.3).

questions.q: Under the Chicago Convention framework that GACAR implements, ICAO Annexes contain —
questions.explain: ICAO Annexes set out international Standards and Recommended Practices (SARPs) that Contracting States, including Saudi Arabia, implement through national regulation such as the GACARs.

questions.q: A GACAR requirement that differs from the corresponding ICAO Standard must be —
options: Filed as a difference with ICAO
questions.explain: Contracting States must notify ICAO of any difference between their national regulations and ICAO Standards, so operators know where Saudi Arabia's rules diverge.

banks.title: Pilot Licensing & Ratings
banks.desc: Eligibility ages, medical classes, currency, flight reviews and instrument recency under GACAR Part 61.

questions.q: What is the minimum age to be eligible for a GACAR private pilot certificate for a rating other than a glider or balloon?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.133 requires an applicant to be at least 17 years of age for a private pilot certificate in other than a glider or balloon.

questions.q: What is the minimum age for a GACAR commercial pilot certificate?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.153 requires a commercial pilot applicant to be at least 18 years of age.

questions.q: What is the minimum age to be eligible for an airline transport pilot (ATP) certificate under GACAR?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.173 requires an ATP applicant to be at least 23 years of age and of good moral character.

questions.q: To be eligible for a student pilot certificate (for other than a glider or balloon), an applicant must be at least —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.103 sets the minimum age for a student pilot certificate at 16 (14 for a glider or balloon).

questions.q: Which class of medical certificate must a person normally hold when exercising the privileges of a student pilot certificate?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.105 requires at least a Class 2 medical certificate for a student pilot, except a student training to become a sport pilot need only hold at least a Class 3.

questions.q: An applicant for an ATP certificate must hold at least which class of medical certificate (except for glider/balloon operations)?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.173 requires an ATP applicant to hold a Class 1 medical certificate, except glider or balloon operations need only a Class 2.

questions.q: Within what period must a required flight crew member have completed a flight review to act as a required crew member of an aircraft?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.21 prohibits acting as a required flight crew member unless a flight review has been satisfactorily completed in the 24 months before the month of flight.

questions.q: A standard GACAR flight review consists of, as a minimum —
options: 1 hour of flight training and 1 hour of ground training
1 hour of flight training and 3 hours of ground training
questions.explain: GACAR §61.21 specifies a flight review as a minimum of 1 hour of flight training and 1 hour of ground training.

questions.q: To act as PIC of an aircraft carrying passengers, a pilot must have made at least three takeoffs and three landings within —
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17 requires three takeoffs and three landings within the preceding 90 days to carry passengers, in the same category, class and type.

questions.q: Under GACAR §61.17, to act as PIC under IFR a pilot must, within the preceding 6 months, have performed and logged at least —
options: Six instrument approaches, holding procedures, and course intercepting and tracking
One instrument approach and one hold
questions.explain: GACAR §61.17(a)(3) requires, within the preceding 6 months, six instrument approaches plus holding procedures and intercepting/tracking courses, flown solely by reference to instruments.

questions.q: Which English language proficiency level is the minimum required for eligibility for most GACAR pilot certificates?
questions.explain: GACAR §61.53 requires an ability equivalent to or greater than Operational Level (Level 4) of the Language Proficiency Rating Scale in Appendix A.

questions.q: A holder of a foreign pilot licence from an ICAO Contracting State may be issued which Saudi Arabian certificate under GACAR §61.55?
options: A Saudi Arabian commercial pilot certificate
A Saudi Arabian private pilot certificate with appropriate ratings
A Saudi Arabian flight instructor certificate
questions.explain: GACAR §61.55 allows a person holding a foreign licence at private pilot level or higher from an ICAO Contracting State to be issued a Saudi Arabian private pilot certificate with the appropriate ratings.

questions.q: What is the minimum age to be issued a Private Pilot Licence (aeroplane) under GACAR Part 61?
questions.explain: GACAR Part 61 sets the minimum age for a PPL (aeroplane) at 17, in line with ICAO Annex 1.

questions.q: What is the minimum total flight time required for a PPL (aeroplane)?
questions.explain: A PPL (aeroplane) requires at least 40 hours of flight time under GACAR Part 61.

questions.q: A student pilot flying solo may:
questions.explain: Student-pilot privileges under GACAR Part 61 do not permit carrying passengers.

questions.q: To carry passengers, a pilot must have made at least how many take-offs and landings in the preceding 90 days?
questions.explain: Recency of experience under GACAR Part 61 requires 3 take-offs and 3 landings in the preceding 90 days.

questions.q: Which licence is the minimum required to be paid to act as a pilot?
questions.explain: A Commercial Pilot Licence (CPL) is the minimum to exercise pilot privileges for remuneration or hire.

questions.q: A separate class rating is generally required to act as pilot-in-command of:
questions.explain: Class ratings (e.g. single-engine vs multi-engine) are required under GACAR Part 61 for different classes of aircraft.

questions.q: A type rating is required to act as PIC of —
options: Large aircraft, turbojet-powered aircraft, and other aircraft GACA specifies
Any aircraft with more than two seats
questions.explain: Type ratings are required for large/turbojet aircraft and other aircraft GACA specifies, in addition to the appropriate category/class rating.

questions.q: A GACAR pilot certificate itself does not expire, but exercising its privileges requires —
options: A current medical certificate and satisfying recency-of-experience requirements
questions.explain: Most GACAR pilot certificates don't expire, but exercising their privileges requires a valid medical certificate and meeting recency requirements.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 61, ratings that may be added to a pilot certificate include —
options: Category, class, type and instrument ratings
questions.explain: GACAR Part 61 allows category, class, type and instrument ratings to be added to an existing pilot certificate.

questions.q: Before acting as PIC of an aircraft, GACAR Part 61 requires a pilot to hold —
options: The category and class rating appropriate to that aircraft
A type rating for every aircraft regardless of size
questions.explain: A pilot must hold the category and class rating appropriate to the aircraft being flown before acting as PIC.

banks.title: Medical Fitness
banks.desc: Medical certificate classes, vision and hearing standards, special issuance and AME procedures under GACAR Part 67.

questions.q: GACAR Part 67 prescribes the medical standards and certification procedures for which document?
questions.explain: GACAR §67.1 states that Part 67 prescribes the medical standards and certification procedures for issuing medical certificates for airmen.

questions.q: An applicant for a Class 1, 2 or 3 medical certificate must be examined by —
options: An aviation medical examiner (AME) designated under GACAR Part 183
questions.explain: GACAR §67.3 requires the applicant to be examined by an aviation medical examiner (AME) designated in accordance with GACAR Part 183.

questions.q: At the discretion of the GACA medical assessor, the validity of a medical certificate may be extended by up to —
questions.explain: GACAR §67.7 allows the GACA medical assessor, at his discretion, to extend the validity of a medical certificate by up to 45 days.

questions.q: For a Class 1 medical certificate, the required distant visual acuity in each eye separately, with or without correction, is —
questions.explain: GACAR §67.17 requires distant visual acuity of 6/6 (20/20) or better in each eye separately, with or without corrective lenses, for Class 1.

questions.q: For a Class 2 medical certificate, the minimum distant visual acuity in each eye separately (with or without correction) is —
questions.explain: GACAR §67.41 sets the Class 2 distant visual acuity standard at 6/12 (20/40) or better in each eye separately, with binocular acuity of 6/9 (20/30) or better.

questions.q: A person who does not meet the standards of GACAR §§67.17 through 67.27 for a Class 1 certificate may apply for —
options: Discretionary issuance of a certificate under §67.87
questions.explain: GACAR §67.29 allows a person who does not meet §§67.17–67.27 to apply for the discretionary issuance of a certificate under §67.87.

questions.q: Under GACAR §67.87, which document granted to a person with a static, non-progressive disqualifying condition does NOT expire?
questions.explain: GACAR §67.87 states a Statement of Demonstrated Ability (SODA) does not expire and authorizes an AME to issue a medical certificate if the condition has not adversely changed.

questions.q: Examinations for a Class 1 medical certificate may be performed by —
options: Only an AME specifically designated for that purpose
questions.explain: GACAR §67.91 provides that only an AME specifically designated for the purpose may perform examinations for Class 1 medical certificates; any AME may perform Class 2 or 3 examinations.

questions.q: A person denied a medical certificate by an AME may appeal the decision in accordance with —
questions.explain: GACAR §67.93 provides that a person denied a medical certificate by an AME may appeal the decision in accordance with the procedures prescribed in GACAR Part 13.

questions.q: If a holder fails to provide medical information requested by the President under GACAR §67.95, the President may —
options: Suspend, modify, or revoke the medical certificate
questions.explain: GACAR §67.95 allows the President to suspend, modify, or revoke a medical certificate (or deny an application) if the holder fails to provide requested medical information or authorize its release.

questions.q: Under GACAR §67.5, the level of medical fitness required for renewal of a medical certificate is —
options: Lower than for the initial certificate
The same as for the initial certificate, except where otherwise specifically stated
Higher than for the initial certificate
questions.explain: GACAR §67.5 states the level of medical fitness required for renewal is the same as for the initial certificate, except where otherwise specifically stated.

questions.q: The holder of a medical certificate that has been suspended or revoked must —
options: Return it to the President upon request
questions.explain: GACAR §67.97 requires the holder of any suspended or revoked medical certificate to return it to the President upon his request.

questions.q: What is the minimum class of medical certificate for a Private Pilot Licence?
questions.explain: A Class 2 medical certificate is the minimum for a PPL under GACAR Part 67.

questions.q: What class of medical certificate is required for commercial flying (CPL/ATPL)?
questions.explain: Commercial operations require a Class 1 medical certificate under GACAR Part 67.

questions.q: Who is authorised to conduct an aviation medical examination?
questions.explain: Only a GACA-approved Aviation Medical Examiner (AME) may conduct the examination under GACAR Part 67.

questions.q: If a pilot develops a medical condition likely to affect fitness to fly, they must:
options: Keep flying until the next renewal
Not exercise licence privileges until assessed
questions.explain: A pilot must not exercise the privileges of the licence when a condition could make them unfit, pending assessment, under GACAR Part 67.

questions.q: In which GACAR Part are the medical fitness standards found?
questions.explain: Medical standards and certification are contained in GACAR Part 67.

questions.q: An aviation medical certificate is:
options: Valid for a defined period that must be kept current
questions.explain: A medical certificate has a defined validity period that the holder must keep current under GACAR Part 67.

questions.q: A medical certificate issued under GACAR Part 67 is normally valid counting from the date of —
questions.explain: Medical certificate validity is normally counted from the date of the medical examination.

questions.q: Which of the following is specifically identified as a disqualifying medical condition under Part-67-style medical standards?
questions.explain: Epilepsy is specifically listed as a disqualifying medical condition, unless a specific special-issuance waiver applies.

questions.q: If an AME is uncertain whether an applicant meets the medical standards, the AME should —
options: Defer the case to GACA for a decision rather than issue the certificate
Deny it permanently with no appeal
questions.explain: When in doubt, an AME defers the case to the regulator rather than making the final call themselves.

questions.q: The three classes of medical certificate under GACAR Part 67 broadly correspond to —
options: Airline transport / commercial / private pilot privileges
questions.explain: Class 1 supports ATP-level privileges, Class 2 commercial, and Class 3 private-pilot-level privileges — broadly tiered to certificate privileges.

banks.title: Aircraft, Equipment & Airworthiness
banks.desc: Oxygen, transponders, instrument tests, airworthiness directives and maintenance under GACAR Parts 91, 39 and 43.

questions.q: On a non-pressurized aircraft, above which cabin pressure altitude must the required minimum flight crew use supplemental oxygen for the entire flight time at those altitudes?
questions.explain: GACAR §91.223 requires the minimum flight crew to use supplemental oxygen during the entire flight time at cabin pressure altitudes above 13,000 ft MSL.

questions.q: Between cabin pressure altitudes above 10,000 ft and up to and including 13,000 ft MSL, the required minimum flight crew must use supplemental oxygen for that part of the flight at those altitudes that lasts more than —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.223 requires supplemental oxygen for the minimum flight crew when the flight above 10,000 ft up to 13,000 ft MSL exceeds 30 minutes duration.

questions.q: Within what period must an ATC transponder have been tested and inspected for it to be used as required by GACAR Part 91?
questions.explain: GACAR §91.453 prohibits use of an ATC transponder unless it has been tested and inspected within the preceding 24 months and found to comply with Appendix E to GACAR Part 43.

questions.q: To operate an aircraft in controlled airspace under IFR, the altimeter and static pressure system must have been tested and inspected within —
questions.explain: GACAR §91.451 requires each static pressure system, altimeter and automatic pressure altitude reporting system to have been tested within the preceding 24 months to operate in controlled airspace under IFR.

questions.q: Generally, no person may operate an aircraft unless within the preceding 12 months it has had —
options: An annual inspection in accordance with GACAR Part 43
questions.explain: GACAR §91.449 prohibits operating an aircraft unless within the preceding 12 months it has had an annual inspection in accordance with GACAR Part 43 and been approved for return to service.

questions.q: Who is primarily responsible for maintaining an aircraft in an airworthy condition, including compliance with GACAR Part 39?
options: The owner or operator of the aircraft
questions.explain: GACAR §91.443 states the owner or operator of an aircraft is primarily responsible for maintaining it in an airworthy condition, including compliance with GACAR Part 39.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 39, a person may operate an aircraft to which an airworthiness directive applies —
options: Only in accordance with the requirements of that airworthiness directive
questions.explain: GACAR §39.3 prohibits operating an aircraft to which an airworthiness directive applies except in accordance with the requirements of that directive.

questions.q: If there is a conflict between an airworthiness directive and a related service document, the affected person must —
questions.explain: GACAR §39.7 requires that, in the event of conflict between a service document and an airworthiness directive, the affected person must comply with the airworthiness directive.

questions.q: Under GACAR Part 39, a special flight permit may be issued for an aircraft to —
options: Carry passengers for hire despite an open AD
Fly to a repair facility to complete work required by an airworthiness directive, provided it is capable of safe flight
Operate indefinitely with an unsafe condition
questions.explain: GACAR §39.15 allows a special flight permit for an aircraft to fly to a repair facility to complete work required by an AD, provided the aircraft is capable of safe flight.

questions.q: Under GACAR §43.5, the holder of a pilot certificate (other than a sport pilot) may perform which work on an aircraft they own or operate and not used under Part 121 or 135?
questions.explain: GACAR §43.5(g) allows a pilot certificate holder (except a sport pilot) to perform preventive maintenance on an aircraft owned or operated by that pilot and not used under Part 121 or 135.

questions.q: Under GACAR §43.3, an aircraft component may be described as 'rebuilt' only if it has been disassembled, cleaned, inspected, repaired, reassembled and tested —
options: To any standard acceptable to the owner
To the same tolerances and limits as a new item
questions.explain: GACAR §43.3 permits describing an item as rebuilt only if it has been disassembled, cleaned, inspected, repaired, reassembled and tested to the same tolerances and limits as a new item.

questions.q: GACAR §43.19 requires that work done in maintenance, alteration or preventive maintenance leaves the item in a condition —
options: At least equal to its original or properly altered condition
Better than new in all respects
Acceptable to the aircraft owner only
Equal to the minimum equipment list standard
questions.explain: GACAR §43.19 requires the work to be done so the condition of the item is at least equal to its original or properly altered condition with regard to airworthiness.

questions.q: Which instruments are required for VFR day flight?
options: Airspeed indicator, altimeter and magnetic compass
questions.explain: Basic VFR-day equipment under GACAR Part 91 includes an airspeed indicator, altimeter and magnetic compass.

questions.q: An ELT is:
questions.explain: An ELT (Emergency Locator Transmitter) transmits a distress signal to aid search and rescue.

questions.q: Rules for maintenance and return to service are found in GACAR:
questions.explain: Maintenance, preventive maintenance and return to service are governed by GACAR Part 43.

questions.q: The pitot-static system supplies:
options: The airspeed indicator, altimeter and vertical speed indicator
questions.explain: The pitot-static system drives the airspeed indicator, altimeter and VSI.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A blocked pitot tube (with the static port clear) primarily affects the:
questions.explain: A blocked pitot tube directly affects the airspeed indicator.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Continued airworthiness of an aircraft is maintained by:
options: Completing the required inspections and maintenance
questions.explain: Airworthiness is maintained by the inspections and maintenance required under GACAR Part 43.

questions.q: Which of the following is generally required for IFR flight but NOT required for day VFR flight?
options: A gyroscopic attitude/heading system and navigation/radio equipment appropriate to the route
questions.explain: IFR flight requires additional gyroscopic and radio/navigation equipment beyond the basic VFR instrument set.

questions.q: An Airworthiness Directive (AD) is issued when —
options: An unsafe condition exists in a product and is likely to exist in other products of the same design
A new paint scheme is approved
questions.explain: ADs are mandatory corrective actions issued when an unsafe condition is found that likely affects other aircraft/products of the same type design.

questions.q: Preventive maintenance may generally be performed by —
options: The aircraft owner/operator (for permitted items) or an appropriately certificated person
Nobody without a repair station certificate
questions.explain: GACAR Part 43 allows certain simple preventive maintenance tasks to be performed by the owner/operator (if a pilot certificate holder) or other authorized persons, within limits.

questions.q: Maintenance records must be retained to show —
options: The total time in service and the current status of applicable airworthiness directives
questions.explain: Maintenance records must document total time in service and AD compliance status, among other things, so continued airworthiness can be verified.

banks.title: Aviation Weather
banks.desc: Atmosphere, clouds, fronts, thunderstorms, icing and visibility hazards drawn from the FAA Aviation Weather Handbook.

questions.q: What type of cloud is associated with severe turbulence, icing, lightning and hail, and should be avoided?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook identifies cumulonimbus clouds as thunderstorm clouds carrying severe turbulence, icing, lightning and hail - all thunderstorms are hazardous to aircraft.

questions.q: A thunderstorm cell transitions to the mature stage when which event occurs?
options: The downdraft cuts off the moisture supply
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook states a cell transitions to the mature stage when precipitation reaches the surface, creating a strong downdraft alongside the updraft; weather hazards peak toward the end of this stage.

questions.q: In the International Standard Atmosphere, what are the sea level temperature and pressure?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook gives the standard atmosphere as 15 degrees C (59 degrees F) and 29.92 inches of mercury (1013.25 hPa) at sea level.

questions.q: What is the standard temperature lapse rate in the troposphere?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook lists the standard tropospheric lapse rate as 6.5 degrees C per 1,000 metres, which is about 3.57 degrees F - roughly 2 degrees C per 1,000 feet.

questions.q: Which surface weather measurement is most useful for anticipating the formation of fog?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook notes the surface temperature-dewpoint spread is important in anticipating fog; when the spread reaches zero the air is saturated and fog can form.

questions.q: Radiation fog forms primarily under which conditions?
options: Strong winds and overcast skies over water
Clear skies, light winds and moist air over land at night
Warm air moving over a cold sea surface
Air being forced up sloping terrain in daytime
questions.explain: Per the FAA Aviation Weather Handbook, radiation fog is favoured by a shallow moist surface layer, clear skies and light surface winds; it is a nighttime land occurrence caused by terrestrial cooling.

questions.q: How does advection fog form?
options: Moist air moving over a colder surface and cooling below its dewpoint
Nighttime radiational cooling of the ground
Rain evaporating into cold air beneath a front
Air being lifted over a mountain ridge
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook defines advection fog as forming when moist air moves over a colder surface and is cooled below its dewpoint; it is common along coasts and is called sea fog at sea.

questions.q: A microburst is best described as which type of weather hazard?
options: A small-scale, intense downdraft - the most severe type of wind shear
A persistent layer of stratus cloud
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook describes a microburst as a small-scale, intense downdraft that spreads outward at the surface and is the most severe type of wind shear, with downdrafts up to 6,000 fpm.

questions.q: Which structural icing type is rough, milky and opaque, and is the most frequently reported icing type?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook describes rime ice as rough, milky and opaque ice formed by instantaneous freezing of small supercooled droplets, and notes it is the most frequently reported icing type.

questions.q: Which force, caused by the Earth's rotation, deflects moving air to the right in the Northern Hemisphere?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook explains the Coriolis force is an apparent force from the Earth's rotation that deflects air to the right in the Northern Hemisphere, acting at a right angle to wind direction.

questions.q: What effect does stable air generally have on the vertical motion of air?
options: It has no effect on vertical motion
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook states atmospheric stability affects vertical motion: stable air suppresses vertical motion, while unstable air enhances it.

questions.q: The arc-shaped leading edge of cool, gusty downdraft air spreading out ahead of a mature thunderstorm is called what?
questions.explain: The FAA Aviation Weather Handbook calls the arc-shaped leading edge of downdraft air a gust front; uplift along it can trigger new cells ahead of the parent cell.

questions.q: A sea breeze typically blows:
options: From land to sea by day
From sea to land by day
questions.explain: By day the land heats faster than the sea, so the surface wind (sea breeze) blows from sea to land.

questions.q: Fog forms when:
options: Air is heated above its dew point
Air is cooled to its dew point
questions.explain: Fog forms when air is cooled to its dew point and water vapour condenses near the surface.

questions.q: Structural icing requires:
options: Visible moisture and a temperature at or below freezing
questions.explain: Airframe icing needs visible moisture and a temperature at or below 0°C.

questions.q: A temperature inversion is a layer in which:
questions.explain: In an inversion, temperature increases with altitude, often trapping haze and stabilising the air.

questions.q: Wind shear is:
options: A sudden change in wind speed or direction over a short distance
questions.explain: Wind shear is a rapid change in wind velocity over a small change in position, hazardous near the ground.

questions.q: A METAR is:
options: A forecast for the next 24 hours
A routine, observed aviation weather report
questions.explain: A METAR is a routine observation of current conditions at an aerodrome (a TAF is the forecast).

questions.q: A TAF (Terminal Aerodrome Forecast) is —
options: A scheduled forecast of expected weather conditions at an aerodrome for a specified period
A record of past observed weather
questions.explain: A TAF is a forecast (not an observation) of expected aerodrome weather over a defined valid period.

questions.q: Convective SIGMETs are issued for hazards including —
options: Severe or embedded thunderstorms and tornadoes
questions.explain: Convective SIGMETs warn of severe convective weather hazards such as severe/embedded thunderstorms, tornadoes, hail and severe turbulence.

questions.q: Katabatic (drainage) winds occur when —
options: Cold, dense air flows downhill under gravity, typically at night
Warm air rises rapidly during the day
questions.explain: Katabatic winds form as cold, denser air drains downslope under gravity, most commonly overnight.

questions.q: Clear-air turbulence (CAT) is most commonly associated with —
options: Strong wind shear near the jet stream
questions.explain: CAT typically occurs in strong wind-shear zones around the jet stream, often without visible cloud to warn pilots.

banks.title: Aerodynamics & Aircraft Systems
banks.desc: Forces of flight, stalls, load factor, airspeeds and powerplant systems from the FAA Pilot's Handbook and Airplane Flying Handbook.

questions.q: What are the four forces acting on an aircraft in flight?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge identifies the four forces acting on an aircraft in straight-and-level flight as lift, weight, thrust and drag.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: An aerodynamic stall occurs when which condition is reached?
options: A specific low airspeed is reached
Engine power is reduced to idle
The wing exceeds its critical angle of attack
questions.explain: The FAA Airplane Flying Handbook states a stall occurs when the angle of attack exceeds the wing's critical angle of attack; it can occur at any airspeed, attitude or power setting.

questions.q: Is it possible to exceed the critical angle of attack and stall the wing at high airspeed?
options: No, stalls only occur at low airspeed
Yes, the critical AOA can be exceeded at any airspeed
Only above maximum structural cruising speed
questions.explain: The FAA Airplane Flying Handbook explicitly states it is possible to exceed the critical angle of attack - and therefore stall - at any airspeed, at any attitude and at any power setting.

questions.q: On a standard airspeed indicator, the lower limit of the white arc represents which airspeed?
options: The power-off stall speed in the landing configuration (VS0)
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states the lower limit of the white arc is VS0 - the stalling speed or minimum steady flight speed in the landing configuration with gear and flaps down.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: What does the red line on a standard airspeed indicator indicate?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states the red line marks VNE, the never-exceed speed; operating above it is prohibited as it may cause structural damage or failure.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: The yellow arc on an airspeed indicator represents which range?
options: The caution range, to be used only in smooth air
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge describes the yellow arc as the caution range, to be flown within only in smooth air and then only with caution.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Design maneuvering speed (VA) is best described as which of the following?
options: The maximum speed at which the design's limit load can be imposed without structural damage
The best speed for climbing over an obstacle
The maximum speed with flaps extended
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge defines VA as the maximum speed at which the structural design's limit load can be imposed - by gusts or full control deflection - without causing structural damage; it decreases with lighter weight.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Carburetor ice is most likely to form under which conditions?
options: Only in sub-freezing outside air temperatures
Temperatures below 21 degrees C (70 degrees F) with relative humidity above 80 percent
High temperatures with very dry air
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states carburetor ice is most likely when temperatures are below 70 degrees F (21 degrees C) and relative humidity is above 80 percent, though it can occur even at higher temperatures.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Adverse yaw causes an aircraft to yaw in which direction during a turn entry?
options: In the same direction as the bank
Opposite to the direction of the bank
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge explains adverse yaw results from extra drag on the rising (outside) wing, so from the pilot's perspective the yaw is opposite the direction of the bank.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: How does an increased load factor affect an aircraft's stalling speed?
options: It has no effect on stalling speed
An increased load factor raises the stalling speed
An increased load factor lowers the stalling speed
It only affects stalling speed below 1g
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge discusses load factors and stalling speeds: increasing load factor, such as in a steep turn, increases the speed at which the wing stalls.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: For a given calibrated airspeed, how does true airspeed behave as altitude increases?
options: True airspeed decreases as altitude increases
True airspeed stays the same at all altitudes
True airspeed increases as altitude increases
True airspeed equals groundspeed at all altitudes
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states that because air density decreases with altitude, for a given calibrated airspeed the true airspeed increases as altitude increases.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: In a spark-ignition reciprocating engine, what is the function of the magnetos?
options: To provide the spark that ignites the fuel-air mixture
To meter air into the carburetor
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge explains the ignition system uses magnetos, spark plugs and leads to provide the spark that ignites the fuel-air mixture in the cylinders.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Aerodynamic lift acts:
questions.explain: Lift acts perpendicular to the relative airflow; drag acts parallel to it.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Induced drag is greatest:
options: At low speed and high angle of attack
questions.explain: Induced drag rises as angle of attack increases, so it is greatest at low speed.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: An aeroplane stalls when it exceeds:
questions.explain: A stall is caused by exceeding the critical angle of attack, and can occur at any airspeed or attitude.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: In a level turn, load factor:
questions.explain: As bank angle increases in a level turn, load factor (and stall speed) increases.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Ground effect causes:
options: Increased induced drag near the surface
Reduced induced drag near the surface
questions.explain: Within about one wingspan of the surface, induced drag is reduced, which can cause floating in the flare.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: As aircraft weight decreases, manoeuvring speed (Va):
questions.explain: Va decreases as weight decreases, because a lighter aircraft reaches limiting load factor at a lower speed.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: An aircraft's center of gravity (CG) location primarily affects —
questions.explain: CG position strongly affects an aircraft's longitudinal (pitch) stability and how it handles/controls.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Adverse yaw during a turn entry is best countered by —
options: Coordinated use of aileron and rudder
questions.explain: Coordinated rudder use alongside aileron input counteracts adverse yaw when entering/exiting a turn.

questions.q: P-factor (asymmetric propeller loading) is most pronounced —
options: At high angles of attack and high power settings
At idle power on the ground
questions.explain: P-factor increases with high angle of attack and high power, contributing to a yawing tendency.

questions.q: Torque effect from engine and propeller rotation tends to roll the aircraft —
options: Opposite to the direction of propeller rotation
In the same direction as propeller rotation
questions.explain: By Newton's third law, engine/propeller torque rolls the airframe opposite to the direction the propeller turns.

banks.title: Human Factors & Aeromedical
banks.desc: Hypoxia, carbon monoxide, spatial disorientation, fatigue and aeronautical decision-making from FAA aeromedical and risk-management references.

questions.q: Oxygen starvation from hypoxia affects which part of the body first?
questions.explain: The FAA Hypoxia brochure states oxygen starvation first affects the brain so judgment is impaired - a pilot may not realise they are in trouble.

questions.q: At 18,000 feet MSL, how does the available oxygen compare with sea level?
options: It is the same as at sea level
There is about half the oxygen of sea level
There is about one-quarter the oxygen of sea level
There is more oxygen than at sea level
questions.explain: The FAA Hypoxia brochure notes that at 18,000 feet MSL the air pressure is half that at sea level, so only half the oxygen is available.

questions.q: If a pilot detects an unusual cabin smell suggesting carbon monoxide, what is an appropriate immediate action?
options: Turn the cabin heat fully off and increase fresh-air ventilation
Increase cabin heat to clear the air
Ignore it and continue the flight
questions.explain: The FAA Carbon Monoxide brochure advises pilots to turn cabin heat fully off, increase fresh-air ventilation to maximum, consider supplemental oxygen, and land as promptly as possible.

questions.q: A 'black-hole approach' visual illusion is most likely to occur in which situation?
options: A daytime approach over a city
A night approach over water or unlit terrain to a lighted runway with no visible horizon
questions.explain: The FAA Spatial Disorientation brochure describes the black-hole approach illusion as occurring on a night final approach over water or unlighted terrain to a lighted runway beyond which the horizon is not visible.

questions.q: If a pilot experiences a visual illusion or spatial disorientation in flight, what is the recommended response?
options: Trust bodily sensations over the instruments
Have confidence in the flight instruments and ignore conflicting bodily signals
Close the eyes briefly to reset perception
questions.explain: The FAA Spatial Disorientation brochure advises pilots to have confidence in their instruments and ignore conflicting bodily signals, noting accidents usually result from indecision to rely on instruments.

questions.q: Which type of photoreceptor in the eye is used for low-light and night vision?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot Vision brochure states rods are used for low light intensity or night vision, while cones handle colour and detail in brighter light.

questions.q: For effective visual scanning to detect other traffic, each eye movement should be limited to roughly what arc, observing each area for at least one second?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot Vision brochure recommends a series of short, regularly-spaced eye movements not exceeding 10 degrees, with each area observed for at least one second to enable detection.

questions.q: Altitude-induced decompression sickness is caused by which mechanism?
options: Nitrogen coming out of solution and forming bubbles in the body
questions.explain: The FAA Altitude-Induced Decompression Sickness brochure explains that with reduced pressure, dissolved nitrogen can come out of solution and form bubbles in the body - the condition known as 'the bends'.

questions.q: Why must a pilot allow sufficient time after SCUBA diving before flying?
options: Excess nitrogen stored in the body can trigger decompression sickness at altitudes as low as 5,000 ft
Diving reduces visual acuity for several days
Flying too soon causes carbon monoxide poisoning
questions.explain: The FAA Altitude-Induced Decompression Sickness brochure warns that if not enough time is allowed to eliminate excess body nitrogen after diving, altitude DCS can occur at altitudes as low as 5,000 ft or less.

questions.q: What does the IMSAFE checklist help a pilot assess?
options: Personal fitness for flight - Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue and Emotion
questions.explain: The FAA Risk Management Handbook describes IMSAFE as an acronym for Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue and Emotion, used by a pilot to identify personal hazards before flight.

questions.q: Which of the following is one of the five hazardous attitudes identified in the FAA Risk Management Handbook?
questions.explain: The FAA Risk Management Handbook lists five hazardous attitudes: anti-authority, impulsivity, invulnerability ('It won't happen to me'), macho and resignation.

questions.q: The PAVE checklist is used by a pilot for what purpose?
options: Identifying hazards and managing risk before and during a flight
questions.explain: The FAA Risk Management Handbook presents the PAVE checklist as a tool for risk management analysis, helping the pilot identify hazards to decide on the desirability of undertaking or continuing a flight.

questions.q: The first action for a pilot experiencing hypoxia is to:
questions.explain: Hypoxia is treated by supplemental oxygen and descending to a lower altitude.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Hyperventilation is best corrected by:
questions.explain: Hyperventilation (over-breathing) is corrected by consciously slowing the breathing rate.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Spatial disorientation is best overcome by:
options: Trusting and relying on the flight instruments
questions.explain: When disoriented, the pilot should trust the flight instruments rather than bodily sensations.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: The "IM SAFE" checklist is used to assess:
options: The pilot’s personal fitness to fly
questions.explain: IM SAFE (Illness, Medication, Stress, Alcohol, Fatigue, Emotion/Eating) checks the pilot’s fitness to fly.

questions.q: Carbon monoxide poisoning in a light aircraft most often comes from:
questions.explain: CO can leak from a cracked exhaust into the cabin-heat system; a CO detector and fresh air are key defences.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Which is one of the five hazardous attitudes in aeronautical decision-making?
questions.explain: The five hazardous attitudes are anti-authority, impulsivity, invulnerability, macho and resignation.

questions.q: Fatigue impairs pilot performance primarily by —
options: Slowing reaction time and degrading judgment and decision-making
Having no measurable effect on flying
questions.explain: Fatigue degrades reaction time, attention, and judgment — all critical to safe flying.

questions.q: The FAA's "DECIDE" model for in-flight decision making includes the steps —
options: Detect, Estimate, Choose, Identify, Do, Evaluate
See, Avoid, Report, Log, Wait, Act
Radio, First, Fly, Navigate, Communicate, Land
Aviate, Navigate, Communicate, Stop, Turn, Hold
questions.explain: The DECIDE model structures in-flight decision making as Detect, Estimate, Choose a course of action, Identify solutions, Do the necessary actions, and Evaluate the effect.

questions.q: "Get-there-itis" describes a hazardous mindset in which a pilot —
options: Fixates on completing the flight as planned despite worsening conditions
Refuses to fly under any circumstances
questions.explain: "Get-there-itis" is the hazardous tendency to press on toward the destination despite deteriorating conditions or mounting risk.

questions.q: Situational awareness in flight refers to a pilot's —
options: Accurate perception of the aircraft, environment and their interaction, now and in the near future
Knowledge of the aircraft's paint scheme
Ability to fly without looking outside
questions.explain: Situational awareness is the pilot's accurate, up-to-date mental picture of the aircraft, environment, and how they're likely to evolve.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

banks.title: Navigation & Flight Planning
banks.desc: Charts, the coordinate system, magnetic variation and deviation, VOR navigation and wind correction from the FAA Pilot's Handbook.

questions.q: Lines running east and west, parallel to the equator and used to measure position north or south, are called what?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states circles parallel to the equator are parallels of latitude, used to measure degrees of latitude north or south of the equator.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: From which location is longitude measured as the zero (Prime) meridian?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states the Prime Meridian, which passes through Greenwich, England, is the zero line from which longitude is measured east and west to 180 degrees.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: In aerial navigation, what is the angular difference between true direction and magnetic direction called?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states the difference between true and magnetic directions is called variation; isogonic lines join points of equal variation.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: A line along which there is zero magnetic variation is known as what?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge explains that along the agonic line the two poles are aligned and there is no variation.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Compass deviation is an error caused by what?
options: The difference between true and magnetic north
The aircraft's own localised magnetic influences acting on the compass
Wind drifting the aircraft off course
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge states each aircraft has internal magnetic influences affecting its compass; deviation is corrected using a compass deviation card to obtain the compass course.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Navigating by reference to visible landmarks on the ground is known as what?
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge defines pilotage as navigating by reference to visible landmarks, distinct from dead reckoning and radio navigation.
questions.cite: FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge

questions.q: Dead reckoning is best described as which method of navigation?
options: Computing direction and distance from a known position
questions.explain: The FAA Pilot's Handbook of Aeronautical Knowledge defines dead reckoning as navigation by computations of direction and distance from a known position.
